content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 31, 1990


Mike Downey
Office of the City Attorney
1500 Warburton Avenue
Santa Clara, CA 95050

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-90-369

Dear Mr. Downey:

You have requested advice concerning the responsibilities of
various cities and the County of Santa Clara under the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

Your question originated from telephone conversations with
Margaret Ellison of our staff and myself which was later followed
up with a fax of a joint powers agreement to enable us to more
adequately respond to the verbal question provided on the
telephone. Since you have not, by letter, specifically requested
advice, we consider this to be a request for informal
assistance.2/

QUESTION

Is a public benefit study group comprised of the County of
Santa Clara and the cities located within the county of Santa
Clara subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of
the Political Reform Act?


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Page Two
July 31, 1990


CONCLUSION

It appears that the public benefit study group is solely
advisory in nature and is not subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act.

FACTS

On the telephone you indicated that the County of Santa Clara
and various cities within the county have entered into a joint
powers agreement to conduct a public benefit study on a proposed
sports stadium facility. The group will be addressing such issues
as the economic impact of such a stadium, the cost benefit to the
community, the amount of financial participation by respective
cities, etc. The city and county representatives will be
reporting to their respective jurisdictions with the findings of
the study and may be making recommendations to their respective
legislative bodies regarding specific plans to improve the public
benefit of such a stadium.

The decision to proceed with the stadium project does not
rest with each legislative body but rather will be a ballot
measure to be decided upon by the voters in Santa Clara County at
the November 1990 election.

During our phone conversation you were asked to send a copy
of the joint powers agreement so we could better address this
issue. That agreement provides that the parties to the agreement
"...join together for the purpose of creating recommendations
pertaining to: (a) whether or not a facility should be
constructed in Santa Clara County (City of Santa Clara) through a
benefit study which will identify specific benefits to each city
and general benefits to the county as a whole; and (b) any other
area of inquiry related to such potential facilities deemed
appropriate by the parties hereto...." The agreement continues to
provide that "...the first task of the governing board shall be to
identify the site for the facility."

You indicated to me on the telephone that the site for the
facility was previously determined as land owned by the County of
Santa Clara and that there is no other site within the county
which could accommodate such a stadium.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires every agency to adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code (Section 87300). A
conflict of interest code is a document which designates the
positions within the agency which make or participate in the

Page Three
July 31, 1990


making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect
on any financial interest and further sets forth the types of
investments, business positions, income and real property made
reportable by those designated employees (Section 87302).

The term "designated employee" excludes "...any unsalaried
member of any board or commission which serves a solely advisory
function." (Section 82019, emphasis added.)

Commission Regulation 18700 provides guidance in determining
whether a board or commission is "solely advisory" or has
decision-making authority.

(a) "Public official at any level of state or local
government" means every natural person who is a member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency.

(1) "Member" shall include, but not be limited to,
salaried or unsalaried members of board or
commissions with decision-making authority. A
board or commission possesses decision-making
authority whenever:

(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it
may prevent a governmental decision either by
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridden; or

(C) It makes substantive recommendations which
are, and over an extended period of time have been,
regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or
governmental agency.

Regulation 18700(a)(1)

The stadium study group does not make, compel or prevent any
final decisions. It is merely obtaining statistical information
on the probable benefits the respective jurisdictions may enjoy
should the stadium issue pass the November ballot.

The study group is at present solely advisory in nature.
This advice may change should the measure pass and the group
continue in existence with different functions or obligations.

Page Four
July 31, 1990


I hope this has answered your question satisfactorily.  If
you have any further questions, please feel free to call me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
    Political Reform Consultant
    Legal Division

SH/JET/jt
